speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, there was a lot of them in Poland. And in [INAUDIBLE], did you speak any Polish? No, I never learned Polish. Yiddish was my first language. [INTERPOSING VOICES] My parents were very smart. In Poland, we spoke Yiddish. We came to Israel, we started to speak Polish. [LAUGHS] And still, until they passed away, they spoke only Polish. [INTERPOSING VOICES] September the 14th, 1995. The survivor is Bernice Greenbaum, and Greenberg, her maiden name. Ronald Weinstein, interviewer, Los Angeles, California, USA. It will be in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Ron Weinstein-- W-E-I-N-S-T-E-I-N. Today's date is September 14, 1995. I'm conducting an interview with Bernice Greenbaum, G-R-E-E-N-B-A-U-M. I'm conducting the interview in Los Angeles, California, United States of America. The interview will be conducted in English.

speaker: interviewee
text: Who do I look at, you?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you give me your current name and the spelling of the name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bernice Greenbaum, B-E-R-N-I-C-E. Greenbaum is G-R-E-E-N-B-A-U-M.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you give me your name at birth and spell it for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Brandla, B-R-A-N-D-L-A. Greenberg, G-R-E-E-N-B-E-R-G.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you give me the name and spelling of any other names? Your first marriage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Weisberg. Bernice Weisberg, B-E-R-N-I-C-E. Weisberg is W-E-I-S-B-E-R-G.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any nickname?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: October 29, 1926.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your age?

speaker: interviewee
text: 68. I'm going to be 69.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you give me the name and spelling of the city in which you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zwolen. That's in Poland. It's Z-W-O-L-E-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is Zwolen near any large city in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Is it near any large city in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It's not far from Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you begin by describing what your childhood was like before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I-- Zwolen was a small city, and there were lots of Jewish people. And I was attending school, and it was a Catholic school that we all have to. But I still was going to another school, to Bais Yaakov. Everything I know from Yiddish is from these few years that I attended at Bais Yaakov. I remember that I-- when I was going to school, I was a very good student. And my parents were very proud of me. And that school that I attended was Catholic, so we had to stand up each time when the other children were saying the prayers. But we had a teacher for Jewish religion one hour a day. That was beside that I was going to Bais Yaakov. And our house was very Orthodox. My mother was very Orthodox. And my father wasn't that Orthodox, because he was the president of the Jewish community. And also, the business that we had, he had lots of contact with the Gentile people. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of business did your father have?

speaker: interviewee
text: He had automobiles. And they were transporting people from one city to the other. It was a corporation, and he was the bookkeeper. And those days, to be a bookkeeper in a small city and Jewish people was very prestigious. And also, I can recall the Saturdays and Friday nights. Like, my father always used to bring home [NON-ENGLISH], means somebody who didn't have where to go, or what to eat. And so he used to bring them home, and they were sitting, and we were feeding them. And what I remember from my childhood also is that on Saturdays I used to go with my girlfriend, Eva Young. She survived. We used to go from house to house to ask for, you know, bread, challah, for the poor people. And we used to take it to them. And so really this is what I remember from my childhood before 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any rituals that your family observed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They were like on a-- you mean religious? Oh, yes. They used to go to the synagogue. And my mother used to always go to the mikveh. And-- and it was on Saturdays the synagogue, always that my mother always used to go too. But I don't remember being that-- personally, me-- being very religious, keep all of the long sleeves, the things like that. And that was-- yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that you learned Yiddish. So what languages do you speak?

speaker: interviewee
text: I speak Polish, English, Yiddish. I understand German. I understand Russian too.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what languages did you speak during your childhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yiddish in the house. And Polish, because I was going to school. I was-- when I was starting school, they took examination. And I never went to the first grade, because my sister used to teach me in the house. So each time that they wanted me to read, I already knew it by heart. And that's why I never attended first grade. That's how I finished public school, seven grades in 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your relationship like with non-Jews at the Catholic school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I thought it was-- until like 1938, it was OK. They didn't persecute us, so-- But in 1938, it started-- before 1939, the end of it. They always used to have everywhere the signs, [POLISH]. That means don't buy anything from the Jews. In fact, our automobile, what we had a business, that we-- it was on my father's name. [POLISH] means the license. And in 1938, the end of 1938, we had to change it to another name, [? Masokevitch. ?] It's like somebody we knew, because they didn't give anymore for the Jewish people concessions like this stuff that they can operate.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you know about the rise of the Nazis in Germany during your childhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. We didn't know anything about Nazis in Germany. The only thing that started out is in September, 1939. That is when the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any warnings, or did you have any discussions about impending war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. The only thing is they probably did, but I just didn't notice it that much. Because they mobilized my brother, Chil. They mobilized him to the army right the first day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the members of your family at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother, Chil, my sister, Dwoire, and my brother, Joseph, my Walwale, little brother. And I had another sister. Her name was Gina, and she lived in-- and she worked in Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the rest of you lived together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you when war broke out in 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1939, I remember playing in the backyard, and I was perfect. And the 1st, September 1st, is when it broke out. In September 6 is when they already bombarded our city. And these airplanes were flying so low that you could even see these faces. And this was when it started, right away, in September 6. They bombard. The whole city was on fire. So this is when the hard life begins for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did your family do at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we-- these automobiles, they mobilized all of them. They took them away from us and they gave us a piece of paper that someday they're going to pay us for it, if they win the war. And so we-- one of the them was left because it didn't have any gasoline.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we took that automobile and our family and my father's brother also, with the children, and strange people. And we went to-- we just ran away from the-- from the bombs.

speaker: interviewee
text: And wherever we went, these bombs, they were just following us. We went to Lublin. They were bombarding them. Pulawy. And I was almost out of my mind. They said, a little longer. I was so scared. I was screaming all the time. I almost-- and--

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you able to take with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. I wound up with one dress and with-- the dark shoes. Well, because it was, you know, the fire. The whole city was burning. It was a small city. But I remember when we were at night, traveling, we could see the fire from the city. And we were traveling so long. Then they stopped in Lutsk and they were bombarding there. And then we went to-- we wound up in Rovno. That was in 1939. It must have been a few days, this traveling. It didn't take long. And the Russians came in over there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before that, it was Poland. And then became-- there were lots of [NON-ENGLISH]. That's called-- what do you call them? Refugees, hundreds of them. And we didn't have where to go, so we slept and stayed in the synagogue in Rovno. And this is when my grandmother died, in Rovno. And what did your grandmother die of?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember what she died of. She just died. Maybe from-- she was old and there was no food. And dirt, so many people. The way they were arranging this like a blanket was dividing one family from the other when we were asleep. And at that time the Russians were there, and they sent out almost everybody to Russia, including us. And-- not very far. Not Siberia, or anything. It was like near [INAUDIBLE]. Not so far. And when we came, they were very nice. They-- they greeted us with music, and they gave us good to eat for the first week. And that was it. Later on, we didn't even have anything good to eat. And everybody has to work hard. And then my sister got acquainted with some officer, and she was begging him. The biggest mistake in our lives, what we did is to go back to Rovno. Because it was Russia, so we could with-- with-- he helped, that officer.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Russian officer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that we should go back. And that's the biggest mistake in our lives, because they would have been alive now. My father, every day he used to say this, 'The biggest mistake of our lives.' And that wasn't-- and then we came back. There was no place where to work for my brothers, so they went to Ternopil for work. And my father was working. There was a big building with bricks and very high, about two or three stories. And he had to climb and take them apart, just the-- and still, I used to go to sleep very hungry. At night, in the meantime, I attended a little bit of that school for learn Russian. Not very long, because then the Germans came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: What time period are we talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm talking about-- that was already 1940. '41 is when the Russians came in. That's my recollection. I think that's what it was. I mean the Germans came in.

speaker: interviewee
text: Can I get? And-- [DRINKS] Well, this is when, well, the real trouble started out for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they-- at that time, we moved out to Warkowicze. When the Germans came in, they already bombed Warkowicze. That was about maybe 10 miles or 15 miles from Rovno.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did your family move?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Warkowicze? Because over there there was no place where to stay. You couldn't find even a place where to stay. You had to stay in the synagogues. And in Warkowicze we could find at least where to say even though-- And then the Germans came in. This is when we really found out about the Jewish people, what they're doing. They congregated all of them and put them all together, like a ghetto. And I went to-- I don't remember what you call this in English-- Wioska, in a village very close to-- to-- to Warkowicze. And I started to work the-- I started to work in the village attending to the cows-- in the pastures--

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you able to escape the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I somehow just-- somebody-- I think that somebody from the-- from the-- Ukraine-- not Ukrainian. Gentiles helped me. And I started to work there. But it didn't take long that they were liquidating the ghetto. Oh, I forgot to say that-- oh, yeah. And they liquidated the ghetto. While I was on the pasture, you know, with the cows, I noticed from far away some people. And I could still see it from my eyes, and it was unbelievable. And this was my mother, father, and sister, and my little brother escaped from the ghetto when they liquidate it. To liquidate the ghetto and [POLISH] and this part of the country was that they took all these people-- just like cattle, they were. And they took them out of the city. And they-- they had to dig graves, and they were walking on a-- on a board, and they were shooting them. And they were just falling into these big graves. For many weeks that earth was just going up and down because of the blood and all that. Some children, they were tossed in alive because they wanted to save the money on these bullets. And so they escaped at that time. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your family manage to escape liquidation?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just want to say, they just-- lots of people ran and lots of people got killed. And some of them, they just made it through the fields. But I forgot also to say that I was in a send-out before all this. I was sent out to Arbeitslager. Not the concentration camp, but the Arbeitslager. And it was called Tynne, T-Y-N-N-E. And I was there not very long. And they liquidated-- one night, they were going to liquidate this labor camp. And one of the Germans knocked at a window and he said we should run because they're going to kill us. And we actually ran to the forest. I remember there was about eight of us, the girls that I knew. And when we ran to the forest, the Ukrainian police, they were running after us. And they were shooting. They shot them all.

speaker: interviewee
text: But when it came to me, I looked over and I said, why do you have to shoot me? I have gold. Because that's what my father and mother did. They gave everybody whatever we had. Jewelry. And I said, I give it to you. You don't have to shoot me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, well, give it to me. So I did. And he said, run, I'm going to shoot in the air. I couldn't believe that he meant it. And he actually shot in the air, and this is how I-- that's the first time that  I survived. And at that time-- because I forgot, at that time my mother and father were still in the ghetto. And they already were crying. They thought that I was already dead. And right after this is when they liquidated the ghetto. And then I said that when my-- I saw these people, and that was my mother, father, and sister, and brother. And I ran to them, and they came over. And I went to these people that I worked for to ask them if they can hide there. And they said no. That I could, because I don't look Jewish. And so I said, well, whatever is going to happen to them is going to happen to me. So I left with them. That's how our-- This is how our journey began together.

speaker: interviewer
text: If we can return for just a moment to the labor camp. What kind of a job did you have at the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I remember, I didn't have any shoes and I was walking on the gravel. And all my fingers were bloody, underneath the fingers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your toes, you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, the toes. And at that time, they even put some bandages and some gauze. And that was before that he knocked at the window to run away. They were putting gravel on the railroad. They had a railroad there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall what your daily routine was like at the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we used to get up real early, and very little to eat. But this was not the concentration camp at that time. That was still in 19-- I think that was 1941. And, well, that's all we had to do is work all day. Just keep taking these-- the gravel and just keep emptying them out. And go back and get it and just emptying it out again.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so you were reunited with your family after they escaped the liquidation.

speaker: interviewee
text: After they escaped the liquidation, yes, I was reunited with them. That's how our journey began. And we used to go and-- we're not very familiar with this part of the country. And so we always used to go at night. When we hear the dogs are barking, then we knew that some people lived there. So that's how we used to go. And once I remember we went that was-- we fell into a ditch and all our clothes froze to us. And we knocked at the doors. We used to beg to let us in. I cannot forget that I was begging, and I said, we're going to pay you for a piece of bread. They didn't want to open for me, but a little kitten was meowing, and they opened to let that little kitten in.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, well, we survived there too. We used to go begging for a piece of bread. And many gave us and many didn't.
